concerns.text: The plan defines 'Ground Truth' as bugs confirmed by '≥2 independent human reviewers' or 'linked issues'. However, the dataset strategy relies on 'prs-v2-sample' and 'authors_merged' datasets which typically contain raw PR metadata and comments, not explicit 'reviewer count' flags or verified bug labels. If the ground truth is constructed by heuristically inferring 'confirmation' from the presence of a comment or issue link (as implied by the 'Gap Handling' section), the 'Human' baseline is not an independent ground truth but a noisy proxy. This creates a circular validation where the LLM is compared against a 'Human' signal that is definitionally derived from the same noisy metadata (comments/issues) used to construct the 'Human' bug list, rather than an external, verified bug report. The 'Human' signal is not independent of the 'LLM' signal's input context (the PR text).

The alignment metric (FR-012) requires 'Cosine similarity of bug descriptions' AND 'Jaccard(line_set)'. The LLM is prompted to generate a 'description' of the bug. If the LLM hallucinates a bug location, the Jaccard score will be low. If the LLM hallucinates a description that matches a human comment's text but is in the wrong location, the similarity might be high but Jaccard low. However, if the 'Human' bug list is derived from comments that *also* contain descriptions, the 'description' used for similarity is not an independent ground truth but a text string from the same PR context. The plan risks validating the LLM's ability to mimic human *comment phrasing* rather than detecting actual code defects, especially if the 'Human' bug list is just a list of comments flagged as 'bug reports' without external verification.

McNemar's test is proposed to compare detection rates. This test requires a 2x2 contingency table of paired observations (LLM detected/Human detected, LLM detected/Human missed, etc.). The plan's 'Triangulated Ground Truth' (FR-011) defines the 'Human' baseline as a constructed set. If the 'Human' baseline is not a set of *independent* human annotations but a derived set from the same PR data, the 'paired' nature of the test is compromised. The test assumes the 'Human' detection is a fixed, independent truth. If the 'Human' detection is noisy or derived from the same source as the LLM's input, the statistical significance may be an artifact of the data construction method rather than a true difference in detection capability.

FR-011 states a bug is ground truth if '(a) linked to a GitHub issue AND (b) confirmed by at least two independent human reviewers'. This is a tautological definition of 'ground truth' in the context of the available data. Most open-source PR datasets do not contain a field 'confirmed_by_two_reviewers'. If the plan infers this from the *number of comments* on a PR, it assumes that 'more comments = confirmed bug', which is not a valid scientific proxy. A bug fix might have one comment; a style nitpick might have ten. This definition makes the 'Ground Truth' dependent on the *volume* of human activity rather than the *validity* of the bug, introducing a severe confounding variable.
